county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1832 from Creek Cession. County seat Talladega. Named for Battle of Talladega (1813, Creek War). Talladega College (1867, African American). Talladega Superspeedway (1969). Silk Stocking District has antebellum homes.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Talladega. Talladega County records are intact from 1832 (formed from Creek Cession). The county is named for the Battle of Talladega (1813) during the Creek War. Talladega was the location of the first meeting of the Alabama Presbyterian Synod. The Talladega Superspeedway has brought economic activity since 1969. Talladega College (founded 1867) was one of Alabama's earliest African American colleges — its archives have significant African American family records. The Silk Stocking District in Talladega has antebellum homes. The Talladega County Historical Association has local materials. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds, probate, and marriages. ADAH holds county tax records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Talladega County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.record_losses: 1864 courthouse fire during Civil War; records partially reconstructed
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1832 from Creek Cession. County seat Dadeville. 1864 courthouse fire during Civil War destroyed records; partially reconstructed. Horseshoe Bend National Military Park (1814 Creek War battle). Lake Martin (Martin Dam, 1926).
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Dadeville. The 1864 courthouse fire during the Civil War destroyed records and they were partially reconstructed — this is a burned county. Tallapoosa County was formed from Creek Cession in 1832. Horseshoe Bend National Military Park, site of Andrew Jackson's decisive 1814 victory over the Red Stick Creek, is in the county. Lake Martin (created 1926 by Martin Dam) covers former farmland and Cherokee/Creek sites. Check ADAH and FamilySearch for surviving records. The Dadeville area was an important cotton-growing center. The Tallapoosa County Historical Society has local materials. For Creek War-era records, check NARA military files.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Tallapoosa County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1818 from Cherokee and Creek Cession — one of Alabama's original counties. County seat Tuscaloosa. State capital 1826-1846. University of Alabama (1831). W.S. Hoole Special Collections Library. Bryce Hospital (1861) patient records at ADAH.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Tuscaloosa. Tuscaloosa County records are intact from 1818 — one of Alabama's original counties with excellent record survival. Tuscaloosa served as Alabama's state capital from 1826 to 1846. The University of Alabama (founded 1831) has the W.S. Hoole Special Collections Library with extensive manuscript collections, including planter family papers, Civil War letters, and Alabama history materials. The Tuscaloosa County Preservation Society has local materials. The Jemison-Van de Graaff Mansion has planter family records. Bryce Hospital (Alabama State Hospital for the Insane, opened 1861) has patient records at ADAH — useful for family research. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm coverage. The Tuscaloosa Genealogical Society maintains research files. ADAH holds county tax records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Tuscaloosa County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.record_losses: 1865 Union troops destroyed courthouse and records during Civil War
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1823 from Tuscaloosa County. County seat Jasper. 1865 Union troops destroyed courthouse and records. Strong Unionist area — 1st Alabama Cavalry, USA (Union) recruited here. Coal mining (Bankhead mines) dominated post-war economy.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Jasper. The 1865 Union troops destroyed the courthouse and records during the Civil War — this is a burned county. Walker County was formed in 1823 from Tuscaloosa. Despite being in Alabama, Walker County was a strong Unionist area — the First Alabama Cavalry, USA (Union) recruited heavily from Walker and surrounding counties. This means military records should be checked on both sides. Coal mining became the dominant industry after the war — Bankhead mines and company town records document mining families. The Walker County Heritage Foundation has local materials. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving records. Check ADAH for state-level copies.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1860 courthouse fire destroyed early records; county is one of Alabama's oldest
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1800 from Mississippi Territory — one of Alabama's oldest counties. County seat Chatom (previously St. Stephens and McIntosh Bluff). 1860 courthouse fire destroyed early records. St. Stephens was capital of Alabama Territory (1817-1819). Choctaw heritage.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Chatom. The 1860 courthouse fire destroyed early records — this is a burned county. Washington County was created in 1800 from Mississippi Territory, making it one of the oldest counties in Alabama. The original county seat was at McIntosh Bluff, then moved to St. Stephens (the first territorial capital), then to Chatom. St. Stephens was the capital of Alabama Territory (1817-1819). The county has a diverse history including Choctaw Indian heritage (Treaty of Fort Confederation site). Washington County is heavily forested with significant timber industry records. The Washington County Historical Society has local materials. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving records. For earliest records, check Mississippi territorial records at NARA.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1819 from Dallas and Monroe counties. County seat Camden. Wealthy Black Belt plantation county on the Alabama River. Camden was an important river port. Gee's Bend quilting community. High enslaved population percentage before Civil War.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Camden. Wilcox County records are intact from 1819. The county was one of the wealthiest Black Belt plantation counties before the Civil War, with enslaved persons comprising the vast majority of the population. Camden was a major Alabama River port and commercial center. The Wilcox Historical Society has local materials. Gee's Bend (Boykin) is famous for its quilting community — the isolated African American community has deep genealogical roots traceable through Freedmen's Bureau records and plantation documents. FamilySearch has microfilm of deed books, probate records, and marriages. ADAH holds county tax records. Estate inventories are critical for African American family research.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Wilcox County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1850 from Walker County. County seat Double Springs. Famous as the 'Free State of Winston' — held neutrality convention in 1861, many refused Confederate service. Some joined 1st Alabama Cavalry, USA (Union). Mountainous Appalachian terrain.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Double Springs. Winston County records are intact from 1850. The county is famous as the 'Free State of Winston' — it held a neutrality convention in 1861 and many residents refused to fight for the Confederacy. Some Winston County men joined Union Alabama regiments (1st Alabama Cavalry, USA). This makes Winston County unique for Civil War research — check both Confederate and Union military records. The county's mountainous terrain and Appalachian culture produced distinctive settlement patterns. The Looney's Tavern site marks the neutrality meeting location. The Winston County GenWeb and Natural Bridge of Alabama are local resources. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds, probate, and marriages.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Winston County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants
